html:
Maria Aurora von Königsmarck, born April 28, 1662, at Agathenburg Castle (near Stade), and died February 16, 1728, in Quedlinburg, was a Swedish countess, known among other things for being the mistress of Augustus the Strong. In 1696, she gave birth to Moritz of Saxony, who was his son.

== Biography ==

Aurora Königsmarck was the daughter of Conrad Christoff von Königsmarck (1634–1673), Imperial Master of the Wardrobe, and Maria Christina Wrangel (daughter of the renowned Field Marshal Herman Wrangel of Skokloster), and sister to Philip Christoffer von Königsmarck, Amalia Wilhelmina von Königsmarck, and Carl Johan von Königsmarck. She was the niece of Otto Wilhelm von Königsmarck.

She grew up in Germany and, under her mother's guidance, received a very good education. She spoke German, French, Italian, and Swedish, and had some knowledge of Latin. She loved music and theater, and wrote poetry herself. She was also skilled in embroidery and drawing. Early on, she was recognized for her spirituality and beauty.

In 1680, she moved to Stockholm with her mother. Influenced by French salon feminism, she played a prominent role in the court's social life and helped introduce French classicism to Sweden.

During the winter of 1683–1684, the Swedish premiere of Racine's *Iphigenie* was performed by a group of female amateurs at the court, under the patronage and encouragement of Queen Ulrika Eleonora. Johanna Eleonora De la Gardie played Iphigenie, Amalia Wilhelmina Königsmarck played Achilles, and Ebba Maria De la Gardie played Eriphile. Aurora Königsmarck's role in the play has been incorrectly stated as the title role, and sometimes as Agamemnon. Augusta Wrangel actually played Agamemnon, while Königsmarck played Clytemnestra. This was likely the first play performed exclusively by women in Sweden. She also advocated for women's right to education.

She became a godmother to and close friend of Maria Aurora von Spiegel, a former Ottoman slave who had been brought to Sweden by Aurora's brother, Philip Christoffer.

Her mother died in 1691. The forced reductions and legal proceedings within the family had taken a toll on their finances, and in 1692, Königsmarck traveled from Sweden with Maria Aurora von Spiegel to find happiness elsewhere. In the first few years, she lived in various places in Germany – Hamburg, Stade, and Hanover. During these years, many suitors proposed marriage to the beautiful Königsmarck, but she rejected them all. She had fallen in love with Baron Claes Gustaf Horn during her time in Stockholm. He had been forced to leave Sweden after a fight and was now traveling around Europe. They occasionally crossed paths, and Horn would often ask her for money.

In the autumn of 1694, Königsmarck traveled to Dresden to find out what had happened to her brother, Philip Christoph. There, she met Elector Augustus the Strong of Saxony, became involved in a wild social life at court, and became Augustus' mistress. In 1696, she gave birth to her son, Moritz (Moritz of Saxony, Marshal of France).

She then lived alternately in Dresden, Leipzig, Hamburg, and Quedlinburg, where in 1700 she became the headmistress of the convent of Quedlinburg.

Around New Year's Day in 1702, she traveled to King Charles XII of Sweden's headquarters at Würgen in Courland. This was to try to persuade the king to restore the estates that her family had lost during the reductions. However, the king refused to receive her. During Charles XII's stay in Altranstädt in 1706–1707, Königsmarck made another attempt, but again she was not received by the Swedish king. Her attempts to see Charles XII are the subject of Snoilsky's poem "Aurora Königsmark," which is included in the famous collection *Swedish Pictures*.

She continued to travel around, and her financial situation worsened. She died in Quedlinburg, and her embalmed body was displayed in the castle church until the early 20th century.

== See also == Augustus the Strong [1]

== Bibliography == Collected literary works by Swedish authors from Stjernhjelm to Dalin. Volume 8, featuring Märta Berendes, Ebba Maria and Joh. Eleonora De la Gardie, Amalia Wilh. and Maria Aurora von Königsmark, Thorsten Rudeen, and Carl and Ulrik Rudenschöld. Uppsala: Hanselli. 1867. Pages 69-131. Libris 600769. https://runeberg.org/svsf/8/0073.html

== References == Burton, Nina (2005). The New Women's City: Pioneers and Forgotten Women Over Two Thousand Years. Stockholm: Bonnier. Libris 9863954. ISBN 91-0-010770-0 Löfgren, Lars (2003). Swedish Theatre. Stockholm: Natur och kultur. Libris 9212494. ISBN 91-27-09672-6

=== Notes ===

== Further Reading == Beckman, Margareta (1998). Aurora von Königsmarck: A Swedish Woman Among the Leaders of Europe in the Age of Great Powers. Stockholm: Svenskt militärhistoriskt bibl. Libris 7796535. ISBN 91-972209-4-9 Borgström, Karin (1986). "The Most Famous Woman of Two Centuries." Strong Women: A Selection of Remarkable Female Lives from the Radio Series 'Värt att veta' (1986): pages 59-69. Libris 9559459 Maria Aurora von Königsmarck. An aristocratic woman's life in Baroque Europe. Cologne 2015. IS

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the Age of Liberty is well-documented through parliamentary publications, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by later political biases.
tldr: Aurora Königsmarck was a Swedish noblewoman from the 17th century who became known for her interest in theater and culture. She later became the mistress of the German prince Augustus the Strong.
lattlast:
Aurora was born in Germany but moved to Stockholm as a child. She was highly educated, spoke several languages, and was an important figure in the royal court's entertainment scene. She also advocated for women's education and participated in one of the first theatrical performances in Sweden featuring only female actors.

Due to significant financial losses suffered by her family as a result of political decisions, she left Sweden to seek her fortune in Germany. There, she became known for her beauty and later had a son with the German prince August the Strong. She also traveled to Sweden several times to try to reclaim her family's lost possessions from King Charles XII, but he refused to meet with her.

Aurora died in the city of Quedlinburg in 1728. Her life and her attempts to reach the Swedish king have been romanticized and distorted in poems and literature throughout the centuries.
